maps.text: Part of map of Asia highlighting the region from Mesopotamia to the Zagros Mountains in Iran, representing the areas occupied by the ancient Sumerians (Kessler, P. L., , Map of Sumer). The area is marked approximately to provide the reader with a clearer visualization

common.text:
Writing and Myths

The creation of writing systems not only allowed the description of facts but also the creation of myths (). On this page, we list the kings considered mythological in the Weld-Blundell prism (). Why mythological kings? We consider these kings mythological for the following reasons:

• No evidence other than the prism has been found to prove the existence of these kings ().
  • Reign periods that are impossible for human beings ().
  • The presence of a sentence written using a figure of speech (): "Then the flood swept over. After the flood had swept over, and the kingship had descended from heaven" ( ; ).

Due to the frequent confusion with the literal interpretation of the Mesopotamian flood, we wrote a serie of four texts to help readers to undertstand what ancient authors were describing:

• : we study the concepts of "world" and "sky" in Mesopotamian cultures and how the lack of understanding of the surrounding environment may have led to the idea of an extraordinarily long lifespan, incompatible with human nature.
  • : we explore the water cycle and the law of conservation of mass. With this, we discussed how the concept of "world" in Mesopotamia differs from our own.
  • : we examined the epics of Gilgamesh and Atrahasis, as well as the Sumerian deluge narratives. Within them, we identified a recurring narrative pattern: a few people and animals are saved in a boat, described in various shapes (cubic or circular), following a divine punishment. From this, we established the probable periods in which these deluges occurred and compared them with data from other regions, concluding that there were no global floods.
  • : we concluded our study with an analysis of two different papers that mapped floods across the world at various times, providing context for the conditions under which Mediterranean authors wrote their texts.

Figura 2: Sumerian King List. © Ashmolean Museum, University of Oxford ()

exercise_v2.question: Has the historical existence of all the kings listed in this section of the Sumerian King List been verified?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: There is no archaeological evidence to support the existence of any of these kings. Moreover, one of them is a Sumerian god.

exercise_v2.question: Did the kings really 'descend from heaven'?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: As demonstrated in the section 'Texts with Sciences,' this description should be understood as a metaphor, as the Sumerians had a completely different conception of the sky and the earth. Therefore, we understand that this text cannot be a literal account of historical facts.
